_cite.reason: URL-normalization 1c: strip trailing markdown-emphasis `*`/backtick glued to citation URLs in intelligence_item_sections.content_md (customer-checkable link + validate_item_provenance criterion-2 exact-compare). Operator ruling 2026-07-04. Same transform as the write-site fix.

prior.content_md:
This section addresses the package-level substantive requirements as they apply to the workspace across its three transport modes. Component-level detail is in separate intelligence items.

### 8a. Air Mode — SAF Blending Mandate and Cost Pass-Through

ReFuelEU Aviation (Regulation (EU) 2023/2405) requires aviation fuel suppliers at EU airports to blend minimum SAF levels starting at 2% from 2025, rising to 6% by 2030, 20% by 2035, 34% by 2040, 42% by 2045, and 70% by 2050. Fuel suppliers are the regulated entities. Airlines procuring fuel at EU airports bear the cost differential between SAF and conventional jet fuel, which is then passed through to freight rates.

- **Cause:** SAF blending mandate increases the cost of aviation fuel at EU airports.
- **Mechanical consequence:** Airlines increase freight rates or introduce SAF surcharges to recover incremental fuel costs.
- **Effect on air-primary verticals (live events, fine art, luxury goods, film and TV, high-value automotive, humanitarian):** Cost pass-through on all air freight moving through EU airports. Quantum of pass-through requires carrier-specific data. Humanitarian verticals may face exemption or offset mechanisms depending on carrier and contract structure — this requires carrier-level confirmation.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/2405 (ReFuelEU Aviation), OJ L, 18 October 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/2405/oj/eng*
*Source: EUR-Lex summary — ReFuelEU Aviation. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/EN/legal-content/summary/refueleu-aviation-sustainable-air-transport.html*

### 8b. Road Mode — HDV CO₂ Standards and Infrastructure

Regulation (EU) 2019/1242 as amended by Regulation (EU) 2024/1610 sets CO₂ emission performance standards for new heavy-duty vehicles. The 2024 amendment strengthened targets: 45% reduction for new HDVs by 2030 (vs 2019 baseline), 65% by 2035, and 90% by 2040. These obligations fall on vehicle manufacturers, not on the workspace as a buyer of road capacity.

- **Cause:** Manufacturers must meet fleet-average CO₂ targets.
- **Mechanical consequence:** Accelerated transition to lower-emission or zero-emission HDVs in the EU trucking fleet.
- **Effect on road-secondary operations:** No direct compliance obligation for the workspace. Indirect effect is that road carriers investing in ZEV fleets may incorporate transition costs into freight rates. The workspace may be asked by clients to preference carriers with lower-emission fleets as part of CSRD Scope 3 reduction strategies.

AFIR (Regulation (EU) 2023/1804) mandates public EV charging and hydrogen refuelling deployment across the EU core road network. AFIR obligations fall on EU Member States and infrastructure operators.

- **Effect on road-secondary operations:** No direct compliance obligation for the workspace. Operational relevance is that AFIR-driven infrastructure deployment expands the geographic viability of zero-emission road freight, which may become relevant to client sustainability commitments and Scope 3 strategies.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2019/1242 as amended by Regulation (EU) 2024/1610. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/legal-content/EN/TXT/?uri=OJ:L_202401610*
*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1804 (AFIR). https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1804/oj/eng*

### 8c. Ocean Mode — EU ETS Maritime and FuelEU Maritime

**EU ETS maritime extension:** From 2024, shipping companies operating vessels of 5,000 GT and above on voyages to, from, and between EU ports are subject to EU ETS allowance obligations. The phase-in is 40% of verified emissions in 2024, 70% in 2025, 100% from 2026.

- **Cause:** Shipping companies must surrender EU ETS allowances for covered emissions.
- **Mechanical consequence:** Carbon cost is incorporated into ocean freight rates on EU routes.
- **Effect on ocean-tertiary operations serving EU trade lanes:** Directional cost increase on EU ocean freight rates. Quantum requires carrier-specific data. Effect on cargo verticals varies by route — EU inbound and outbound ocean lanes are affected; purely Asia-Americas ocean lanes with no EU port call are not.

**FuelEU Maritime** (Regulation (EU) 2023/1805): From 1 January 2025, ships of 5,000 GT and above using EU ports must comply with GHG intensity limits on energy used. The regulated entity is the shipping company. The workspace's exposure is cost pass-through and, for large clients subject to CSRD, Scope 3 emissions data requirements on ocean legs.

*Source: Regulation (EU) 2023/1805 (FuelEU Maritime), OJ L 234, 22 September 2023. https://eur-lex.europa.eu/eli/reg/2023/1805/oj/eng*
*Source: EC DG CLIMA — Shipping decarbonisation. https://climate.ec.europa.eu/eu-action/transport-decarbonisation/reducing-emissions-shipping-sector_en*

### 8d. Cross-Mode — CSRD Scope 3 Data Supply Obligations

The CSRD (Corporate Sustainability Reporting Directive) requires large EU undertakings to report Scope 3 Category 4 (upstream transportation and distribution) emissions as part of their European Sustainability Reporting Standards (ESRS) disclosures. Large undertakings subject to CSRD will request transport emissions data from the workspace as their freight forwarder. This is not a direct CSRD obligation on the workspace (unless the workspace itself meets the large-undertaking threshold), but it is a commercial obligation: clients who cannot obtain Scope 3 transport data from their forwarder will move freight to forwarders who can supply it.

- **Cause:** CSRD requires large undertakings to report Scope 3 transport emissions.
- **Mechanical consequence:** Large-undertaking clients request per-shipment or per-lane emissions data from the workspace.
- **Effect on all cargo verticals:** Workspaces that cannot produce verified, methodology-consistent transport emissions data risk losing large-undertaking client mandates to competitors who can. The harmonised transport emissions calculation regulation (COM(2023) 441 final, political agreement November 2025) will define the required methodology once in force.

*Source: COM(2021) 550 final, Section 2.2.1, European Commission, 14 July 2021.*
*Source: European Commission DG MOVE — Political agreement on transport emissions calculation harmonisation. https://transport.ec.europa.eu/news-events/news/commission-welcomes-political-agreement-new-rules-harmonise-transport-emissions-calculations-eu-2025-11-06_en*

---
